title: 第十一講
en: Language, Myth, Customs and Laws, Sacred Books.

TTAVING first determined by means of definition ±± the exact limits of Natural Religion, and having afterwards explained the reasons why the Historical Method seems to be the most advantageous for a truly scientific treatment of the religions of the world, we have now to find out what materials there are accessible to us from which to study the growth and decay of Natural Religion in the widest sense of the word.
These materials may be divided into four classes.
First comes language, which in its continuous growth leads us back to the earliest periods of thought, or, at all events, to periods which cannot be reached by any other kind of evidence.
The second class is formed by what it is the fashion to call mythology, which, as I shall show, is really an inevitable phase in the development of language and thought.

The third class of evidence comprises religious customs and laws, which may be studied either in historical documents, or by actual observation of such MATERIALS FOE THE STUDY OP NATURAL RELIGION. 281 customs and laws as are still prevalent among civilised as well as uncivilised races.
The fourth class consists of the /Sacred Books of the great religions of the world.
Language as Evidence.

If, as I hope to show, every word was originally a deed, was, in fact, a creative act, calling into life a concept which did not exist before, it will sound less surprising that it is possible to discover in words, taken by themselves, a record of the most primitive thoughts of mankind. It is true that a dictionary by itself conveys no meaning, and that it is only in a sentence that words become significant. But we know now that originally every word was a sentence. When a man said sar-it, river, he really said, 'running (s a r) here (it)'; when he said dar-u, tree, he said, 'splitting (da r) here (u).' But men who called their trees ' splitting here,' or what is split, must have been men who had learnt to use trees for certain purposes, and who probably possessed some tools, however rude, to help them in carrying out their work. Men who called their horse a quick runner, as-va, equus, Ittitos, must have been men to whom the horse had become useful as a runner, for there were many wild animals quicker than the horse, though they were not even singled out for a name, but were comprehended under the general term of wild animals.
You will see now how, if we can but find an entrance into the ancient workshop of language, we can still listen there to the earliest thoughts of man. But where is that workshop ?
In order to answer that question, I shall have to 282 LECTURE XI.

devote some of my next lectures to giving you a short account of the discoveries made by the students of the Science of Language. That science has opened before us a new world, and it will be necessary for me to place before you a map of that new world, though in the broadest outline only, in order that you may be able to watch the earliest migrations, not only of language, but of thought, of myth, of religion, and of law and custom.
Survey of Languages. Aryan Family.
Let us begin with Europe, and in Europe with England1.
English.

Have you ever asked yourselves what it means that we speak English, what a language is, what the English language is, where it sprang up or how it was made, and how it came to be spoken in these distant isles, and from thence again over nearly the whole civilised world ?

Nothing seems to me so wonderful as the power which man possesses of ceasing to wonder at what is most wonderful. It has been said with great truth that a sign or wonder can never exist twice, for when it happened the second time we should call it quite natural, and cease to wonder at it. Some philosophers go even further and maintain that a sign or wonder ceases to exist the moment it does exist, because whenever it exists, there must have been a sufficient 1 I have left here this short survey of languages, which I found it necessary to give in my first course of lectures, in order to avoid the necessity of explaining again and again the names and the relationship of the languages in which the religions of the world found their expression. Readers who require fuller information, may consult my Lectures on the Science of Language.

MATEKIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 283 reason for it, and whatever has a sufficient reason, ceases to be wonderful. Well, whatever the reason may be, we certainly all of us seem to have acquired what Orientals consider a proof of the highest breeding, namely to wonder at nothing, to be surprised by nothing, the old Nil admirari.

Here we find ourselves in a small island, adjacent to what is a mere promontory of the vast Asiatic continent. And in this small island which we call Great Britain, and in this mere promontory which we call the Continent of Europe, we speak a language which is to all intents and purposes the same as that which is spoken in Ceylon, an island adjacent to the southern promontory of the same Asiatic continent, called the Dekhan or Southern India.

This discovery of the unity of language in India and England is only about a hundred years old, and when it was first announced, it startled some of the most learned and judicious men to that extent that Dugald Stewart, for instance, declared it was an utter impossibility, and that Sanskrit must be an invention of those arch-deceivers, the Brahmans, who wanted to make themselves as good as ourselves, and as old as ourselves ; nay, a great deal better and a great deal older too.

We have recovered from that surprise, and we find now at the beginning of most Latin and Greek grammars a few paragraphs about the Indo-European or Aryan family of speech, and a statement that much may be learnt from Sanskrit, the sacred language of the inhabitants of India, as to the antecedents of our own language, and as to how Latin and Greek became what they are.

But there are still greater miracles in English such as we find it spoken at the present day, if only we had eyes to see and ears to hear them. English is said to consist of 250,000 words, and most of these words are capable of ever so many changes which we call declension, conjugation, degrees of comparison, composition, and all the rest. That is to say, there is ready made for every one of us an instrument with at least several millions of keys on which we play, as if it were a pianoforte with ninetysix keys.

When uncivilised people hear an organ for the first time, they generally feel a curiosity to open it, to see how it acts, and what it is made of. But this gigantic organ which we call our language, we never try to open, we never ask how it was made or who made it. No, we take it for granted or given, and we think we may thump and hammer on it to our heart's content, trusting that it will always remain in tune.
Veda, 018a.

But though the relationship between the languages of India, Persia, Armenia, Greek, Latin, Celtic, Slavonic, and Teutonic has now become part and parcel of the general stock of knowledge, it is seldom realised how close that relationship really is. It is known that the roots of all these languages are the same, that their grammatical articulation is the same, that a number of important words, such as the numerals, names for father, mother, sky, sun and moon, horse and cow, are the same. But it was only a study of Sanskrit, and of the most ancient, the Vedic Sanskrit, which enabled scholars to discover that MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 285 so mysterious a change as that which we observe, for instance, in the vowels of to ivit, to know, and / ivot, I know, or in German in Ich weiss, I know, and Wir wissen, we know, has its first cause in a change of accent which took place in the most ancient Sanskrit. We must remember that the accent exists, or, at all events, is marked in Vedic Sanskrit only, that it was in fact unknown to scholars till the Veda began to be studied, and we shall then understand what it means that a change of accent observed in Sanskrit three thousand years ago, still determines the vowels of words which we use to-day. I wot is the AS. wdt, the Gothic wait, I know. We have the infinitive preserved in the phrase to wit. This wit is the Sanskrit vid, to know. From it is formed in Sanskrit a perfect ve'da, having the meaning of the present, just like the Greek olba, i. e. Faida. The change of i into ai or e is due to the accent, which in Sanskrit falls in the singular on the first syllable. This diphthong ai in Sanskrit, ai in Gothic, becomes regularly a in AS., and o in English.

But that is not all. Why did the Greeks say otba in the singular, but lo-^v in the plural ? In Greek the accent does not move, it remains throughout on the first syllable. But in Sanskrit the accent which is on the first syllable in the singular, must migrate in the plural to the last syllable. Why it did so, is a question difficult to answer, but the most natural reason seems to be that the differentiating terminations in the plural continued to be felt as such, and therefore retained their stress longer than those of the singular. Hence we say ve'da, vettha, veda, but vidmas, vida, vid us. This rule and this rule alone enables 286 LECTURE XL us to account for la-fiev in ancient Greek, for Teh weiss and Wir wissen in modern German.

This will give you an idea of the solidarity, as the French call it, that binds the languages, and, if the languages, then the thoughts of all the members of the Aryan people together. And now as to their various degrees of relationship.
Anglo-Saxon.

English, as now spoken, may be traced back in one uninterrupted line to Anglo-Saxon. Of Anglo-Saxon we have the earliest documents in the seventh century, such as the Beowulf, an ancient epic of Teutonic origin. The language in which that poem is written was brought to England, or rather to the British isles, by emigrants and conquerors who came from the Continent. They were, as you know, Jutes, Saxons, and Angles, and they all spoke, not High German, but Low German. Low German does not mean vulgar German, but the German spoken in the low-lands of Germany. This Low German is in fact one of the four principal branches of the Teutonic class of the Aryan family, the other branches being Gothic, Scandinavian, and High German.
Gothic.
Gothic was spoken on the Danube in the fourth century, and it has left us the oldest specimens of Teutonic speech, the translation of the Bible of Ulfilas, who died in 381.
Continental Saxon.

Low German comprises the Saxon of the Continent, preserved to us in the Heljand, a poem of the ninth MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 287 century ; the Anglo-Saxon, which we have already mentioned ; the Old Frisian, known to us by documents of the thirteenth century, and slowly dying out at the present day; and lastly the Old Dutch, or Low Franconian, of which we have specimens in the socalled Carolingian Psalms, ascribed to the ninth century, and which is afterwards represented by Middle Dutch, Modern Dutch, Flemish, and the spoken Low Franconian.
Scandinavian.

The third branch, the Scandinavian, is represented by the Old Scandinavian literature between 800 and 1000 A.D., and is divided into (1) West-Nordish, i.e. Icelandic, and Norwegian, with a literature dating from the eleventh century ; and the East-Nordish, that is, Swedish and Danish.
The ancient literature of Iceland, the two Eddas and numerous Sagas, will be of great importance to us for mythological purposes.

These three branches have one common characteristic feature, they are all under what is called 'Grimm's Law,' that is to say, to put it broadly, they offer an aspirate where the other Aryan languages have a tenuis, they offer a tenuis where the others have a media, and they offer a media where the others have an aspirate.

We must not suppose, because Gothic is in so decided a minority, as compared to Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Celtic, or Slavonic, that therefore its aspirate is a corruption of a more ancient tenuis, or its media a corruption of a more primitive aspirate, or its tenuis a corruption of a former media. Looked upon as merely phonetic corruptions, such changes as t to th, th to d, and d to t in one and the same language would defy 288 LECTURE XT.

all principles of phonetic .science. Gothic is as old and as independent a national dialect of Aryan speech as Sanskrit, and, as such, had as much right to fix on tenuis, aspirate, and media for the discrimination of certain roots as Sanskrit had in fixing on media, tenuis, and aspirate. Thus the three roots which appear in Sanskrit as tar, rlhar, and d a r, would from the beginning appear in Gothic as thar, dar, and tar, but one and the same language would never change tar into tha r, dhar into da r, and dar into tar. We know Gothic at a later time than Sanskrit, but that does not make Gothic a less primitive language than Sanskrit. And what applies to language, applies to mythology also. We know Vedic mythology at a much earlier date than Teutonic mythology, but that does not prove that the names and characters of the Teutonic gods were borrowed from the Veda.
Thorr and Thursday.

It is quite true, for instance, that if we want to know the original meaning of the Icelandic god Thorr, we have to trace back that word to the Anglo-Saxon Thunor, the modern thunder. It is true also that we have only to replace th by t, in order to be able to identify thunor with the Latin ton-are. But that does not prove that the Teutonic god Thorr, who still lives in the name of Thursday, dies Jovis, was not as old a god as any of the Vedic deities, and that from the very beginning he did not thunder with an initial aspirate, instead of an initial tenuis.
Tyr and Tuesday.

If we apply Grimm's Law, we generally begin with what we are accustomed to call the classical languages, MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELTGION. 289 Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin. If therefore we find Dyu, nom. Dyaus in Sanskrit, Zevs for Aytvs in Greek, Iu-jpiter for Dyu-piter in Latin, we trace them back to Gothic, Icelandic, Anglo-Saxon, in fact, to Low German, by simply replacing the media by the tenuis. This gives us the Icelandic Tyr, which is preserved in Ti/sdagr, dies Martis, and in Tuesday, the AngloSaxon Tiwesdceg. But all this gives us no right to treat Tyr as a later corruption of the Vedic Dyaus.
Wodan and Wednesday.

Comparison, no doubt, helps us in discovering the origin of the names of the Aryan gods, and as the ancient mythology of the Veda is more richly developed, or, at all events, has been more carefully preserved than that of any other Aryan race, we generally look upon the Sanskrit names as the most primitive. But historically this is a false position. We may, for instance, derive the name of the Teutonic god Wodan or Odin from a Sanskrit root which, if we replaced d by dh, would be vadh, to strike. From it we have the Vedic vadh-ar, thunderbolt, the AngloSaxon iveder, storm and weather, and from it we may guess the original purport of Wod-an to have been the god of the thunderstorm, who still lives in the name of Wednesday, as W6dnes-dceg. But there is no god in the Veda who could be represented as the exact prototype of Wodan, though there are several Vedic gods running parallel to him, just as the Gothic language runs parallel to Vedic Sanskrit.
High German.
Distinct from these three branches of the Teutonic class is the fourth, the High German, which as a rule 290 LECTURE XI.

represents classical tenuis by media, classical aspirate by tenuis, and classical media by an aspirate. In other respects, however, High German is very close to Low German, so that many scholars now group Low and High German together as West-Teutonic, and Gothic and Scandinavian as East-Teutonic.
Old High German is known to us from about 700 to 1100 ; it is then succeeded by Middle High German from 1100 to 1500, and this by Modern High German spoken and written to the present day.
Celtic.

Besides the Low German which took possession of Britain in historic times, chiefly after the fall of the Roman dominion, another branch of Aryan speech overspread these isles in prehistoric times, the Celtic. The Celts too came from the Continent, where we find them migrating from East to West through Gaul and Spain, occasionally bursting into the Balkan and the Italian peninsulas, and sending out one colony as far as Galatia in Asia.

The Celtic class is divided into two branches, the Cymric and the Goidelic. The former comprises Welsh, the extinct Cornish, and the Armorican of Brittany; the Goidelic, the Irish, Gaelic, and Manx. There are besides the ancient inscriptions of Gaul which are sometimes treated as a third branch, the Gallic. Interesting as the Celtic languages are for etjanological and grammatical purposes, their literature is recent, not going back beyond the eighth century a. D. Whatever there is of mythology and ancient religion has evidently passed through a Christian and Romanic filtering, and has to be used MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 291 therefore with extreme caution for comparative purposes1.
Italic.

The next class of Aryan speech which has likewise reached the shores of the British isles, is the Italic. The literary language of Rome was but one of several dialects, elaborated by the Aryas when they settled in Italy. Eesides the Latin we find the Oscan and the Umbrian, and several smaller dialects of which we possess monumental fragments. After reaching its classical culmination, Latin became the lingua vulgaris of the civilised portion of Western Europe, and developed new vulgar and afterwards literary languages in Italy, Gaul, Spain, Portugal, in the Grisons, and, by colonies, in Roumania. We have the earliest documents of French in the ninth century, of Provencal in the tenth, of Italian, Spanish, and Portuguese in the twelfth.

The language of England was touched twice by the waves of the Latin river, the first time through the Roman legions who took possession of Britain, the second time through the Norman conquerors, warriors of Teutonic extraction and Scandinavian blood, who after their conquest of Normandy had exchanged their Teutonic speech for that of Northern Gaul. They brought with them into England a Romanic languao-e, Romanic thought, manners, and tastes, but little of Romanic blood. There may be some Celtic admixture in the Teutonic blood of England ; but the grammar, the blood of the English language, has remained Teutonic throughout.
1 See Professor Rhys, Hibbert Lectures, 1886. U 2 292 LECTURE XI.
Hellenic.

The next class is the Hellenic, And here we must guard against what was formerly a very common view, namely that the Aryas who came to people Greece and Italy were more closely related than the other scions of the Aryan family. Many scholars went so far as to suppose that the ancestors of the Greeks and Romans remained united for a time after they had become separated from the rest. There is no foundation, however, for this hypothesis, at least not so far as language is concerned. Greek shows greater similarity with Sanskrit than with Latin, Latin shows greater similarity with Celtic than with Greek. This is a point of great importance to us in our mythological and religious researches. In historical times the Latin language and the Roman mythology and religion have borrowed so much from Greek that scholars are apt to forget that the borrower was not altogether a pauper, that there was in fact a fully developed religion and mythology in Italy before the contact with Greece, and that it is this prehistoric phase of Italian life which is of chief interest to the student of ancient folk-lore.
The Hellenic class, in its four dialects, the Doric, Aeolic, Attic, and Ionic, is so well known that I need say no more about it in this place.
Slavonic.

We have still one more class of Aryan languages in Europe, the Slavonic, or, as I prefer to call it, the Windic. I prefer the name of Windic, because the oldest name under which the tribes speaking those MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 293 languages became known to us, is not Slaves, but Winidae.
This class is divided into three branches, the Lettic, the South-East Slavonic, and the West Slavonic.
The Lettic comprises (1) the Lettish, now spoken in Kurland and Livonia, the Baltic provinces of Russia. Its literature dates from the sixteenth century.
(2) The Lithuanian, spoken in Eastern Prussia and in Russia, by about a million of people. Its literature dates from 1547, of which date we possess a small catechism.
(3) The old Prussian, which became extinct in the seventeenth century, and left behind a few fragments only of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries.

The South-East Slavonic comprises the old Bulgarian, in which we possess the translation of the Bible, of the ninth century, which is still used as the ' authorised version' ; the Slovenian, Servian, and Croatian (sometimes comprehended under the common designation of Illyrian), with literary remains of the tenth century, and the Russian, the literary language of the Russian Empire.

The West Slavonic consists of the Polish, with a literature dating from the fourteenth century ; the Bohemian, with a literature dating from the tenth century, and the dialects still spoken by Wends and Sorbs in Lusatia.
North- Western Division.
These five classes of Aryan speech which we have hitherto passed in review belong all to Europe, and form together what I call the North-Western division of the Aryan family.

Various attempts have been made to prove that before they became settled in their present seats, some of them remained together for a longer time than the rest, and therefore shared certain features in common which are absent in others. To me it seems that all these attempts have been in vain, and that all the evidence that is brought forward in support of what has been called a genealogical tree of the Aryan languages can be fully accounted for, if we admit that the dialectic varieties which afterwards grew into national languages existed before the Aryan Separation, that whatever forms seemed fittest to this or that clan survived, but that, after the family was once broken up, each dialect went its own way, unconcerned about its neighbours. Every other hypothesis creates as many difficulties as it is meant to solve. That geographical contact has nothing to do with grammatical similarity we see most clearly in Greek and Latin, which, though very close neighbours, are really as distinct as any other two Aryan languages. Celtic shows certain features in common with Latin, Latin with Greek, Teutonic with Lettic, but not one of these casual coincidences requires for its explanation more than the admission of that common dialectic fermentation which preceded here as elsewhere the formation of national languages.
South- Eastern Branch.

It is useful, however, particularly for comparative purposes, to distinguish between those five branches which together form the North -Western division of the Aryan family, and the South- Fa stern division which consists of the languages of India and Persia. There MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 295 is one language which is now supposed to hold an intermediate position between these two divisions, the Armenian, but its exact relationship is still a matter of controversy.

Why this division into a North- Western and SouthEastern branch is useful historically, we shall see when we come to consider the question of what intellectual level had been reached by the Aryan family before they separated. As it is quite clear that in historical times no exchange took place between the Aryas who travelled in a South-Eastern direction to Persia and India, and those who had followed a North-Western direction towards Europe, every word which they share in common, and particularly all words connected with mythology and religion, can be claimed as the common property of the whole Aryan race before its first dispersion.

The languages belonging to this South-Eastern division are of special interest to us, as the principal sacred books are composed in them. Europe has never produced a religion. All religions have their cradle in the East, and the languages of India and Persia have become the vehicles of three of the greatest religions of the world, Bralvniauism, Buddhism, in its three divisions of Southern Buddhism, Northern Buddhism, and (7ainism, and Zoroastrianism. These languages, therefore, will require more careful consideration.
Indie Class. Vedic Hymns.

Let us begin with India. The oldest monument of Indian speech is the Veda. It is curious that wherever we have sacred books, they represent to us the oldest language of the country. It is so in India, it 296 LECTURE XT.

is the same in Persia, in China, in Palestine, and very nearly so in Arabia. How the Veda, which is referred to about 1500 B.C., was preserved to the present day is a kind of fairy story which I must pass by, as we are at present concerned with the history of the language only ; but we shall have to consider it when we come to examine the fourth class of our materials for studying Natural Religion, viz. the Sacred Books. The language of the Veda must of course have been at one time the spoken language of those who composed the Vedic hymns, probably in the NorthWest of India. But in the history of India, that language is always the sacred language, and it possesses words, grammatical forms, and syntactical constructions, unknown in later Sanskrit.
Brahma// as.

The next stage of this language is still Vedic, but whereas the Vedic hymns are all in metre, the next stage shows us the prose of the Brahma?ias, works intended for the elucidation of the Vedic hymns and the Vedic sacrifices. The Sanskrit of these Brahmawas is more settled and regular than that of the hymns, but it still represents a period of language prior to that which is presupposed by the grammar of Pacini, or, what used to be called, classical Sanskrit.
Sutras.

The next phase of Sanskrit is that of the SiUras, which is likewise in some points different from the Sanskrit which Pa?iini would consider as regular, but approaches to it so closely that the chronological interval separating the two can only have been very small.

The whole of this literature, which has been pre- MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 297 served to us in its three stages, is exclusively a priestly literature, and what seems at first sight almost incredible, the whole of it was preserved for a long time by oral tradition only. The hymns must at a very early time have become the subject of the most careful study. Not only every word, but every letter and every accent were settled in the teaching of the schools, and the only marvel is that so many irregular forms should have escaped the levelling influence of teachers from generation to generation. Still, with all its irregularities, the Vedic language, as we know it, has clearly passed through a grammatical discipline, and we actually possess, dating from the third or the Sutra period, a number of treatises, the so-called Pratisakhyas, which show us with what extraordinary minuteness the hymns of the Veda had been analysed.
Pa/'inean Sanskrit.

With the Sutras this stream of Vedic language comes to an end. The famous grammar of Pibiini, which is generally referred to the fourth century B. c, treats the Vedic Sanskrit as already exceptional and antiquated, and presupposes a language and a literature of a different character.

We must never forget that in ancient times literature gives us generally specimens of one dialect only, and that this literary dialect, being lifted out of the living stream of language, becomes what is called classical, that is stagnant and dead. The other non-literary dialects withdraw themselves from our observation, but if after a time a new language rises to the surface and brings with it a new liter- 298 LECTURE XI.

ature, that new language is always a sister dialect rather, and not a direct descendant of the old classical language. The language for which Pacini's rules are intended is not Vedic Sanskrit, but a Sanskrit nevertheless closely allied to it. From Panini's time to the present day that Sanskrit, as a new literary language, has remained perfectly stationary, for the simple reason that any infraction of Pacini's rules, any deviation from the classical type as fixed by him, would have been considered, and is considered to the present day, a grammatical blunder.
Inscriptions of Piyadasi, Third Century B.C.

If we only knew the language of India in these two channels, the Vedic and the Paninean, all would be intelligible. But the marvel is that when for the first time we come across an historical specimen of the spoken language of India, that language is totally different. The first truly historical documents in India are the inscriptions of Piyadasi or Asoka in the middle of the third century B.C. These inscriptions we have now before us as they were written at the time. They contain edicts intended to be understood by the people, and we are safe in supposing that the language in which they are composed must have been, if read out, intelligible to the people.

I cannot describe the state of that language better than by representing it to you as a spoken vulgar dialect of Sanskrit, just as Italian was a spoken vulgar dialect of Latin. Thus, Avhile the Vedic and the Paninean Sanskrit present to us two old dialects, regulated by careful grammatical study and reserved MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 299 for literary purposes, these inscriptions of the third century B.C. represent to us the living dialects of the people, reduced by phonetic wear and tear to a mere ghost of their former self.

And that is not all. While the Sanskrit of the Veda as well as the Sanskrit of Pacini is rendered uniform by rule, the language, as recorded in these inscriptions, allows an unbounded variety, such as would not be tolerated in any purely literary language. We have here the language of India as it was actually spoken in the third century B. c, and its discovery was no small surprise to the believers in one uniform classical Sanskrit.
Buddhist Sanskrit.

Nor is this all. While Brahmanism disdained to use any language but Sanskrit for religious subjects, Buddhism, which was at that time the rising and growing religion of India, availed itself of the spoken dialects in order to influence the great masses of the people ; and so we find that one collection of the sacred writings of the Buddhists, commonly called the Northern, is composed in an irregular dialect, closely resembling the dialect of Asoka's inscriptions, while the second collection, commonly called the Southern, is written in another vulgar dialect, but essentially differing from the former by having evidently received a more careful grammatical polish. The former dialect is generally called the Gdthd dialect, or Mixed Sanskrit, the latter is called Pali, and may be called MdgadM, though it ought not to be confused with the later Prakrit dialect of the same name. These two dialects we can fix his- 300 LECTURE XI.

torically, at least so far that we may assign to the literature, composed in the Gatha dialect, a date anterior to the Christian era, because we have Chinese translations of some of the books of the Northern canon about that time. The text of the Southern canon, after having been handed down by word of mouth, was reduced to writing in 88 B.C.1 What chiefly distinguishes the Southern Pali text from the Northern Gatha text is that the former has clearly undergone a strict grammatical revision, while the latter has not.
Renaissance of Sanskrit Literature.

After the end of the first century A.D., Sanskrit, that is to say, the Pawinean Sanskrit, comes more and more to the front, and we see it used for the ordinary purposes of life, and likewise for public inscriptions. What we generally understand by Sanskrit literature begins about 400 A.D., and to about the same period we may refer the grammatical cultivation of the Prakrit dialects.
Prakrit.

These Prakrit dialects are probably the lineal descendants of the uugrammatical dialects, preserved to us in the inscriptions of Asoka, and again in some of the texts of the Northern Buddhist canon. But whereas at that time they were like wild-growing plants, they have now been trimmed and shorn and regulated by strict grammatical rules, after the pattern of Pacini's grammar. In that form they are used in the Sanskrit plays, much in the same manner 1 Vinaya-pitaka, in Sacred Books of the East, vol. xiii, p. xxxv.

MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 301 as the Italian dialects were used in the Comeclia delle arte, where the Doctor always speaks Bolognese, Arleckmo Bergarnese, Pantaleone Venetian, while the pure Tuscan or Roman was reserved for the Amorosos and Inamoratas1.
Vernaculars.

But again, while the classical Sanskrit and the now equally classical Prakrit remained henceforth stationary, the old springs of language were not stopped, but poured on chiefly in two great channels, the Western and the Eastern, the former represented in our time by Sindhi, Gujarat! , Panjabi, and Western Hindi, the Eastern by Bihari, Bengali, Uriya, and Asami. The Nepali in the North shows more affinity with the Western, the Marathi in the South with the Eastern division.
Sacred Books.

It is necessary to keep this outline of the growth and the ramification of language clearly before our mind, for the Sacred Books with which we shall deal have grown, as it were, on the branches of this tree of speech. We have the hymns of the Veda, the Brahmawas, and Sutras preserved to us in Vedic Sanskrit. We have the Law Book of Manu and the Pura?ias composed in literary Sanskrit, according to Pacini's pattern. We have the Southern canon of Buddhism in Pali, the Angas of the £ainas in old Maharashfri, and the Northern canon of the Buddhists in ungrammatical Prakrit. We shall see that there is even a certain parallelism between the 1 Cf. M. M. , On Bengali, in Report of the British Association for 1847, p. 322.

growth of language and the growth of religion, and that without a knowledge of the historical development of the language many points in the history of the religions of India would remain unintelligible.
Iranic Class.

The last class of the Aryan family which we have still to examine is the Iranic. Here we find much the same phenomena as in India. The most ancient specimen of the language is found in the sacred book of Persia, the Avesta. It is called Zend, which, though it is an entire misnomer, will probably remain the recognised name. It is supposed with considerable probability that this ancient dialect was that of Media rather than of Persia.
Cuneiform Persian Inscriptions.

When, however, we get the first glimpse of the language of Persia in contemporary documents, I mean in the cuneiform inscriptions of Cyrus, Darius, and Xerxes, we find there a language closely allied to that of the sacred writings of Zoroaster, yet different from it. These inscriptions cover the time of the Achaemenian dynasty from about 500 to 336 B.C.
Pehlevi.

Then follows a break of more than five centuries ; but when we meet again with a new literature at the time of the Sassanian dynasty in the first half of the third century a.d., the language, then called Pehlevi, is a decayed Persian, written no longer in cuneiform letters, but in a Semitic alphabet and syllabary. The Pehlevi literature, chiefly concerned with the explanation of the Avesta and with religious questions, lasts MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 303 till about 900 a.d. With 1000 a.d. begins the modern Persian, as we have it in its purity in the great epic of Firdusi, the SMhn&meh, while in later times it becomes more and more mixed with Arabic words through the influence of the Mohammedan religion.

These are the principal languages of the Aryan family, and those which are of special interest to us in the study of religion. There are some other languages, such as Armenian and Ossetian in Asia, and Albanian in Europe, which are clearly of Aryan descent, but which have not yet been referred with perfect precision to any of the great classes of that family. Modern Albanian is supposed to represent the ancient Illyrian. Armenian may constitute a language by itself, more closely related, as shown by Hiibschmann, to the North- Western than to the South-Eastern division.
Bask and Etruscan.

Before we leave the Aryan family, we should still mention two languages, not Aryan in character, but surrounded on all sides by people of Aryan speech, and well-nigh absorbed by them, those of the Basks and the Etruscans.

The Basks, interesting as they are for linguistic purposes, yield us little information with regard to what their ancient religion may have been. The Etruscans, on the contrary, have left us ample materials in monuments and inscriptions, though it must be confessed that not until a really safe key to their language has been discovered, will there be any chance of our understanding the true character of their religion.
Semitic Family.

Quite independent of this enormous stream of language which dominates India, Persia, Armenia and nearly the whole of Europe, there is another stream, the Semitic, running in a bed of its own from the very beginning, and feeding two, if not three of the great religions of the world, that of the Jews, that of the Christians, and that of the Mohammedans.
The Semitic family may be divided into three branches, the Aramaic, the Hebraic and the Arabic, or into two, the Northern, comprising the Aramaic and Hebraic, and the Southern, the Arabic.
Aramaic.

The Aramaic comprises the ancient language of Assyria and Babylon, so far as it has been discovered and deciphered in the cuneiform inscriptions. The grammatical structure of this ancient language is not yet sufficiently made out to enable scholars to trace its exact relation to the later Aramaic. Geographically, however, the ancient language of Mesopotamia may for the present be classed as Aramaic. If some of these cuneiform inscriptions go back, as some scholars maintain, to 4000 B.C., they would represent the oldest remnants of Semitic speech. And if that Semitic literature was preceded, as seems very generally admitted, by another civilisation, not Semitic, and generally called Sumero-Accaclian, we should get an insight into a past more distant than even that which is claimed for Egypt and China. It may be so, but even though chronologically the religious ideas conveyed to us by the sacred hymns of Babylon should prove to be so much earlier than those of any of MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 305 the Aryan races, I must say at once that they appear to me much more advanced, much more modern in point of civilisation. They presuppose towns, temples, idols, a knowledge of metals and all kinds of precious stones, familiarity with writing, and a number of abstract ideas which we should look for in vain in the Vedic hymns. Linguistically also there is little in these inscriptions which we should call much more primitive than what we see in the grammatical structure of Syriac, Arabic, or Hebrew. Many difficulties have here still to be cleared up. An important mine however for religious studies has no doubt been opened there, and several of the antecedents of Hebrew tradition have already been discovered in the cuneiform literature of Babylon. If, as we read, Abraham came from Ur of the Chaldees, his language ought to have been akin to that of the cuneiform inscriptions. But his name and all connected with him passed in later times through the channel of a different language, which we now call Hebrew; and the date at which whatever was known of him was reduced to writingin that form in which we now possess it is still uncertain, but at all events much later than was formerly supposed.
Chaldee and Syriac.

In historical times we find Aramaic spoken in the kingdoms of Babylon and Assyria, and spreading thence into Syria and Palestine. Owing to the political and literary ascendancy of these kingdoms, Aramaic seems for a time to have been a kind of lingua franca extending its influence to Persia, Syria, Asia Minor, Egypt, and even to Arabia.
It has been usual to distinguish between the Ara- 306 LECTURE XI.

maic as used by the Jews, and the Aramaic as used in later times by Christian writers, the former beingcalled Gkaldee, the latter Syriac. It may be true that the name Chaldee owes its origin to the mistaken notion of its having been introduced into Palestine by the Jews returning from the Babylonian captivity. But the name has been too long in possession to make it advisable to replace it by a new name, such as Western Aramaic.

This Jewish Chaldee shows^tself first in some of the books of the Old Testament, such as Ezra and Daniel. Afterwards we find it in the Targums or Chaldee translations of the Pentateuch (Onkelos) and the Prophets (Jonathan), which were read in the Synagogues long before they were finally collected in about the fourth and fifth centuries a.d. The Jerusalem Targums and the Jerusalem Talmud represent the Chaldee as spoken at that time by the Jews in Jerusalem and in Galilee. Christ and his disciples must have employed the same Aramaic dialect, though they also used Greek in addressing the people at large. The conquests of the Arabs and the spreading of their language interfered with the literary cultivation of Chaldee as early as the seventh century ; but it continued to be employed by some Jewish writers down to the tenth century.
The Samaritans translated the Pentateuch into their own Aramaic dialect, which differs but little from that of the Jews.
The Mandaeans also, a somewhat mixed Christian sect in Babylonia, spoke and wrote a Chaldee dialect, which is preserved in their writings and in the jargon of a few surviving members of that sect.

MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 307 Syriac, though spoken long before the rise of Christianity, owes its literary cultivation chiefly to Christian writers. The Old and New Testaments were translated into Syriac (the Peshito) in the second century, and became the recognised text in the school of Edessa and elsewhere. A large literature accumulated from the third to the seventh century, and extended its influence to Persia and the Eastern Roman Empire. The Arabic conquests, however, put an end to the literary cultivation of this language ill aJso, though it lived on both as a written and spoken dialect to the twelfth century, and afterwards, as a language of the learned, to the present day.

The Neo-Syriac dialects, still spoken in some parts of Mesopotamia, chiefly by Nestorian Christians in the neighbourhood of Mosul, and in Kurdistan as far as Lake Urmia, are not directly derived from the literary Syriac, but represent remnants of the spoken Aramaic. One of these dialects has lately received some literary cultivation through the exertions of Christian missionaries.
Hebraic.
The second branch, the Hebraic, comprises Phenician and Carthaginian, as known to us from inscriptions dating from about 600 B.C., and the Hebreiv of the Old Testament.

The Moabites spoke Hebrew, as may be seen from the language of the inscription of King Mesha, about 900 B.C. The Philistines also seem to have spoken the same language, though, it may be, with dialectic varieties. About the time of the Maccabees, Hebrew and its cognate dialects ceased to be spoken by the people at large, though Hebrew remained the language x i 308 LECTURE XI.
of the learned long after the destruction of Jerusalem by Titus. Even at present the Jews employ an artificial and corrupt Hebrew for literary discussions and among themselves.
Arabic.

The third branch, the Arabic, has its home in the Arabian peninsula, where it is still spoken by the bulk of the inhabitants, and from whence it spread over Asia, Africa, and Europe at the time of the Mohammedan conquests. There was a popular Arabic literature long before Mohammed (Mo'allakat), and there are inscriptions in the north of the Hijaz, commonly called Thamudic, which are supposed to be of an ante-Christian date. Arabic inscriptions continue to be found, attesting the use of Arabic as a cultivated language, long before the age of Mohammed. The trilingual inscription of Zabad (Aramaic, Arabic, Greek) dates from 513 A. D. ; a bilingual inscription of Harran (Arabic and Greek) from 568 A. D. A new impulse was given to the literary life of the Arabs by the new religion preached by Mohammed and his successors. The language of the Qur'an became a new type of literary excellence by the side of the ancient Bedouin poetry. In the second century after the Hejra grammatical studies fixed the rules of classical Arabic permanently, and after 1200 years the Qur'an is still read and understood by all educated Arabs. The spoken Arabic, however, differs dialectically in Egypt, Algeria, Syria, and Arabia. One Arabic dialect continues to be spoken in Malta.
Sabaean or Himyaritic.

Iii the South of the Arabian peninsula there existed MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 309 an ancient Sabaean civilisation, remnants of which have been discovered in colossal monuments and in numerous inscriptions, written in a peculiar alphabet, generally called Himyaritic. Their age is doubtful, but some of them are supposed to date from before our era and to extend to the fourth century A.D. It is possible to distinguish traces of different dialects in these Sabaean inscriptions, but they are all closely connected with Arabic. The Sabaean language was probably spoken in the South of the Arabian peninsula till the advent of Mohammedanism, which made Arabia the language of the whole of Yemen.
Ethiopic.

In very early times a colony from Arabia, or, more correctly, from Sabaea, seems to have crossed to Africa. Here, south of Egypt and Nubia, an ancient and very primitive Semitic dialect, closely allied to Sabaean and Arabic, has maintained itself to the present day, the Ethiopic or Abyssinian, or Geez. We have translations of the Bible in Ethiopic, dating from the third or fourth century. Other works followed, all of a theological character.
There are inscriptions also in ancient Ethiopic, dating from the days of the kingdom of Axum, which have been referred to 350, and 500 A.D.
This ancient Ethiopic ceased to be spoken in the ninth century, but it remained in use as a literary language for a long time.

Beginning with the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, a new language appears, the Amharic. In it the Semitic type has been intensely modified, probably owing to the fact that the tribes who spoke it were of Hamitic 310 LECTURE XI.
origin. It is a spreading language, and has given rise in modern times to a new literature.

Other dialects, such as Tigre, EhJdli, and Harrari, so called from the localities in which they are spoken, have not yet been sufficiently explored to enable Semitic scholars to pronounce an opinion whether they are varieties of Amharic, or representatives of more ancient independent dialects 1.

The family likeness of the Semitic is quite as strong as that of the Aryan languages, nay even stronger. Their phonetic character is marked by the preponderance of guttural sounds, their etymological character by the triliteral form of most of the roots, and the manner in which these roots are modified by pronominal suffixes and prefixes ; their grammatical character by the fixity of the vowels for expressing the principal modifications of meaning, a fixity which made it possible to dispense with writing the vowel signs. These characteristic features are so strongly developed that they render it quite impossible to imagine that a Semitic language could ever have sprung from an Aryan or an Aryan from a Semitic. Whether both could have sprung from a common source is a question that has often been asked, and has generally been answered according to personal predilections. Most scholars, I believe, would admit that it could not be shown that a common origin in far distant times is altogether impossible. But the evidence both for and against is by necessity so intangible and evanescent that it does not come within the sphere of practical linguistics.
1 The latest and best account of the Semitic languages is given by Noldeke in the Cyclop. Britannica.

既然我們已經首先通過定義確立了自然宗教的確切範圍，隨後又闡述了為何歷史方法對於世界各宗教的真正科學研究似乎最為有利的理由，我們現在必須尋找那些對我們而言可得取的材料，藉以研究自然宗教在最廣泛意義上的成長與衰落。
這些材料可分為四大類。
首先是語言，在其持續的發展過程中，將我們引領回最早期的思想時代，或者至少可以說，回到無法透過任何其他證據形式所能達及的那些時期。
第二類由所謂的神話所組成，而如我將所示者，這確實是語言與思想發展中一個不可避免的階段。
第三類證據包括宗教習俗與法律，這些可以通過歷史文獻進行研究，或通過實際觀察當今仍在文明與未開化民族中流行的習俗與法律來進行研究。
第四類包括世界各大宗教的《聖典》。
語言作為證據

如果如我所希望展示的那樣，每個詞語原本都是一種行為，實際上是一種創造性的行動，喚起了一個之前不存在的概念，那麼在詞語本身中發現人類最原始思想的記錄是可能的，這聽起來就不那麼令人驚訝了。誠然，詞典本身不傳達任何意義，只有在句子中，詞語才具有意蘊。但我們現在知道，原本每個詞語都是一個句子。當一個人說 sar-it（河流）時，他實際上是說「流動（sar）於此（it）」；當他說 dar-u（樹）時，他說的是「裂開（dar）於此（u）」。但那些把樹木稱為「裂開於此」或「被裂開的東西」的人，必定是學會了為特定目的使用樹木，並且可能擁有某些工具（儘管再粗陋）來幫助他們進行工作的人。那些把他們的馬稱為「快速奔跑者」as-va、equus、ἵππος 的人，必定是那些馬已經成為對其有用的奔跑者的人，因為有許多野生動物比馬跑得更快，儘管這些動物甚至沒有被單獨命名，而是被歸納在野生動物的一般術語之下。
現在你將看出，倘若我們能夠找到一個入口進入語言的古老工坊，我們仍然能夠在那裡傾聽人類最早期的思想。但那個工坊究竟在何處呢？
為了要回答那個問題，我必須
我將在接下來的幾場講座中，為諸位簡要說明語言科學的研究者們所作的發現。該科學已為我們開啟了一個嶄新的世界，為了讓諸位能夠追蹤不僅是語言，更是思想、神話、宗教以及法律與風俗的最早遷徙，我有必要向諸位展示這個新世界的地圖，儘管僅是最廣泛的輪廓而已。
語言概況。雅利安語族。
讓我們從歐洲開始，在歐洲中，又從英國開始1。
我準備好接受英文原文進行翻譯。請提供您要翻譯成繁體中文的英文原文。
你們曾經問過自己，我們說英語意味著什麼嗎？什麼是語言，什麼是英語，它從何而生或是如何形成的，又是怎樣來到這些遙遠的島嶼上，並由此傳播到幾乎整個文明世界呢？

在我看來，沒有什麼比人類具有停止對最奇妙事物感到驚奇的力量更令人驚嘆的了。有人曾極其恰當地說過，奇跡或驚奇之兆永遠不可能出現第二次，因為當它第二次發生時，我們應該稱之為完全自然的事物，不再對它感到驚奇。有些哲學家甚至走得更遠，主張奇跡或驚奇之兆在它存在的那一刻就已經不復存在，因為無論何時它存在，必定曾有充分的 --- **註**：原文在此處有頁注標記「1」，其對應的腳注內容如下： 我在此保留了這份語言簡史，這是我在第一次講座課程中發現必須進行的說明，以避免必須一次又一次地解釋世界各宗教所用語言的名稱與相互關係。需要更詳盡資訊的讀者，可參閱我的《語言科學講座》（*Lectures on the Science of Language*）。
宗教學導論 283 無論有什麼理由，凡具有充分理由之物，便不再是奇異。好吧，不論理由為何，我們確實似乎都已經習得東方人視為最高修養證明的品質，即對任何事物都不驚異、對任何事物都不詫異，亦即古人所謂的「毫無驚異」（Nil admirari）。
我們發現自己置身於一座小島上，毗鄰著廣大亞洲大陸的一個不過是岬角的地區。而在這座我們稱為大不列顛的小島上，以及在這個我們稱為歐洲大陸的不過是岬角的地區，我們所講的語言在各個實際意義上與在錫蘭島上所講的語言相同——錫蘭島乃毗鄰於同一亞洲大陸南部岬角的一座島嶼，該地區被稱為德干（Dekhan）或南印度。
這項發現——即印度與英格蘭語言的統一性——距今僅約一百年，當初首次公佈時，曾使許多學識淵博、判斷審慎的人士感到震驚。例如杜格爾德‧斯圖亞特便宣稱這絕無可能，堅持梵文必為那些大騙子婆羅門的發明，他們想讓自己與我們一樣優越，與我們一樣古老；更有甚者，還想表現得遠遠優越於我們，古老得遠超我們。
我們已從那種驚訝中恢復過來，如今在大多數拉丁文和希臘文文法書的開篇，都能看到關於印歐語或雅利安語族的若干段落，以及一項聲明，即從梵文（印度居民的神聖語言）可以學到許多東西，以瞭解我們自身語言的前身，以及拉丁文和希臘文如何成為今日面貌的過程。
但如果我們有眼睛看、有耳朵聽的話，現代英語中仍存在著更大的奇蹟。據說英語由二十五萬個詞彙組成，而這些詞彙中的大多數都能進行我們所謂的變格、變位、比較級變化、複合詞構成等諸般變化。也就是說，對我們每一個人而言，都現成備妥了一件至少擁有數百萬個按鍵的工具，我們在其上演奏，就如同在有九十六個琴鍵的鋼琴上演奏一般。
當未開化的民族第一次聽到風琴時，他們通常會好奇地想要打開它，看看它是如何運作的，以及它由什麼製成的。但是這個我們稱為語言的巨大風琴，我們卻從不試圖打開它，也從不詢問它是如何製成的，或是誰製造了它。不，我們把它視為理所當然或天成之物，並認為我們可以隨心所欲地敲擊和榔槌它，相信它將永遠保持和諧。

但儘管印度、波斯、亞美尼亞、希臘、拉丁、凱爾特、斯拉夫及日耳曼諸語言之間的親緣關係今日已成為一般知識的公共財產，人們卻鮮少認識到這種親緣關係的真實密切程度。人們知曉這些語言的詞根相同，其文法結構相同，許多重要詞彙，如數字詞、父親、母親、天空、太陽與月亮、馬與牛等詞都相同。然而，正是對梵文的研究，特別是對最古老的吠陀梵文的研究，才使學者們得以發現，像我們在動詞變化中所觀察到的那般神秘的變化—例如在「to wit」（知道）與「I wot」（我知道）的元音變化，或在德文中「Ich weiss」（我知道）與「Wir wissen」（我們知道）的變化—其最初的原因實則源於在最古老的梵文中所發生的重音變化。我們必須銘記，重音僅存在於吠陀梵文中，或至少在吠陀梵文中被標記，直至人們開始研究吠陀之前，學者們對此實為陌生，因此我們便能理解：三千年前在梵文中觀察到的重音變化，仍然決定著我們今日所使用的詞彙中的元音。I wot 是古英文的 wát，哥特文的 wait，意為「我知道」。在不定式短語「to wit」中保留了此詞。此 wit 即梵文的 vid，意為「知道」。在梵文中由此形成的完成式 vedá 具有現在時的含義，正如希臘文的 oĩda（即 *Faida）一樣。i 變為 ai 或 e 乃由重音所致，在梵文中重音在單數形式上落在第一個音節。梵文中的此雙元音 ai、哥特文中的 ai，規律性地在古英文中變為 a，在英文中變為 o。

但事情遠不止於此。為何希臘人在單數時說 ὄψα，在複數時卻說 λόψεν？在希臘語中，重音不移動，自始至終停留在第一音節。但在梵文中，單數時重音在第一音節，複數時則必須遷移到最後一個音節。為什麼會如此遷移，這是個難以回答的問題，但最自然的理由似乎是：複數中的區分詞尾繼續被感受為具有區分作用，因此比單數詞尾保留了更長的重音強度。因此我們說 ve'da、vettha、veda，但 vidmas、vida、vidus。唯有這條規則，並且只有這條規則，才能使我們解釋古希臘語的 λόψεν，以及現代德語中的 Ich weiss 和 Wir wissen。
這會讓你瞭解所謂的「連帶性」（如法國人所稱的）如何將各雅利安民族全體成員的語言，進而將其思想聯繫在一起。現在讓我們轉向它們的各種親緣程度。
盎格魯-撒克遜

當今所說的英語，可以追溯到盎格魯-撒克遜語，其間無有中斷。關於盎格魯-撒克遜語，我們最早的文獻文獻可追溯到七世紀，例如《貝奧武夫》，這是一部日耳曼民族起源的古代史詩。該詩所使用的語言是由移民和征服者帶入英格蘭，或毋寧說帶入英倫群島的，他們來自大陸。如你們所知，他們是朱特人、撒克遜人和盎格魯人，他們講的並非高地日耳曼語，而是低地日耳曼語。低地日耳曼語並非指粗俗的日耳曼語，而是德國低地地區所講的日耳曼語。這種低地日耳曼語實際上是日耳曼語族中四個主要分支之一，該語族隸屬於雅利安語族，其他分支分別為哥特語、斯堪地那維亞語和高地日耳曼語。
哥特語。
哥德語在四世紀時通行於多瑙河流域，它為我們留下了日耳曼語言最古老的標本，即烏爾菲拉斯的聖經譯本，他卒於西元381年。
大陸撒克遜人
低地德語包括大陸撒克遜語，在《海爾揚德》(Heljand)──一部九世紀的詩歌中得以保存；盎格魯—撒克遜語，我們已經提過；古弗里西亞語，由十三世紀的文獻為我們所知，目前正逐漸消亡；最後還有古荷蘭語或低地法蘭克語，我們在所謂的《卡洛林聖詩》中有其標本，該聖詩被推斷為九世紀的作品，而後則由中古荷蘭語、現代荷蘭語、佛蘭德語和口頭低地法蘭克語所代表。
斯堪的納維亞語族。
第三分支為斯堪地那維亞語族，由西元八百至一千年間的古斯堪地那維亞文獻所代表，分為（1）西北日爾曼語（West-Nordish），即冰島語與挪威語，其文獻記載自十一世紀起；以及東北日爾曼語（East-Nordish），即瑞典語與丹麥語。
冰島的古代文獻，包括兩部《埃達》(Edda) 和眾多的《薩迦》(Saga)，對於我們的神話學目的而言，將具有極大的重要性。
這三個分支有一個共同的特徵特性，它們都服從所謂「格林姆定律」(Grimm's Law)，也就是說，粗略地講，它們在其他雅利安語言具有清音之處提供送氣音，在其他語言具有濁音之處提供清音，而在其他語言具有送氣音之處提供濁音。
我們不可因為哥德語在與梵語、希臘語、拉丁語、凱爾特語或斯拉夫語的比較中人數明顯居於少數，就認為它的送氣音是源自更古老的清音的訛變，或其濁音是源自更原始的送氣音的訛變，或其清音是源自更早期的濁音的訛變。若單從音韻訛變的角度來看，在同一種語言中出現 t 變為 th、th 變為 d 以及 d 變為 t 這樣的變化，將難以解釋。288 第 X 講

根據語音科學的所有原則，哥德語作為雅利安語族的民族方言，與梵語一樣古老、一樣獨立，因此同樣有權在清音、濁音與送氣音的選擇上來區別某些詞根，就如梵語在選定濁音、清音與送氣音時所做的那樣。因此，在梵語中呈現為 tar、dhar 與 dar 的三個詞根，從一開始在哥德語中就會呈現為 thar、dar 與 tar，但同一種語言決不會將 tar 改變為 thar、dhar 改變為 dar，以及 dar 改變為 tar。我們認識哥德語的時間比認識梵語要晚得多，但這並不能使哥德語成為比梵語更不原始的語言。而凡是適用於語言的原則，也同樣適用於神話學。我們認識吠陀神話的時間遠早於日耳曼神話，但這並不足以證明日耳曼諸神的名稱與特性是借自吠陀的。
托爾與星期四。

舉例而言，這確實不假：如果我們想要知道冰島神祇托爾（Thorr）的原始意義，我們必須將該詞追溯至盎格魯撒克遜語的圖諾爾（Thunor），亦即現代英語的「雷鳴」（thunder）。同樣地，如果我們將 th 替換為 t，我們確實可以將圖諾爾（thunor）與拉丁文的「ton-are」相同定。但這並不足以證明日耳曼神祇托爾——其名至今猶存於星期四（Thursday，亦即木星日 dies Jovis）之名稱中——不如任何吠陀神祇古老，以及他從一開始就未曾以氣音擦音（initial aspirate）而非清音塞音（initial tenuis）而發出雷鳴。
蒂爾與星期二。

如果我們應用格林法則，通常會從我們習慣稱為古典語言的開始著手——梵文、希臘文和拉丁文。因此，如果我們在梵文中發現 Dyu、nom. Dyaus，在希臘文中發現 Zevs（對應 Aytvs），在拉丁文中發現 Iu-jpiter（對應 Dyu-piter），我們可以將它們追溯到哥德語、冰島語、盎格魯‧撒克遜語，實際上是低地德語，只需將濁塞音替換為清塞音。這給我們帶來冰島語的 Tyr，它保留在 Ti/sdagr（dies Martis）和英文的 Tuesday（盎格魯‧撒克遜語 Tiwesdceg）中。但所有這一切都不足以給予我們理由，將 Tyr 視為吠陀的特尤斯（Dyaus）的後期訛誤。
沃丹與星期三

比較法固然有助於我們發現雅利安諸神名字的起源，而由於吠陀的古老神話發展得更加豐富，或者至少比任何其他雅利安民族的神話保存得更加完善，我們通常將梵文名字視為最原始的。但從歷史角度看，這是一個謬誤的立場。例如，我們可以將日耳曼神祇沃丹（Wodan）或奧丁（Odin）的名字追溯至一個梵文詞根，若我們將 d 替換為 dh，便是 vadh，意為「打擊」。由此而來有吠陀的 vadh-ar（閃電），盎格魯‧撒克遜語的 iveder（風暴與天氣），我們可由此推測沃丹（Wod-an）的原初涵義為雷暴之神，其仍生存於星期三（Wednesday，即 Wódnes-dæg）的名字中。然而，在吠陀中並無任何神祇可被視為沃丹的確切原型，儘管有數位吠陀諸神與他平行相應，正如哥特語之於吠陀梵文一樣。
高地德語。
與日耳曼語族的這三個分支不同的是第四支，高地日耳曼語（High German），這通常是290 第十一講。
用古典清音代表古典濁音，用清音代表古典送氣音，用送氣音代表古典濁音。但在其他方面，高地日耳曼語與低地日耳曼語非常接近，以至於許多學者現在將低地日耳曼語和高地日耳曼語歸為西日耳曼語，將哥德語和斯堪地那維亞語歸為東日耳曼語。
古高地德語（Old High German）的文獻記載自約西元七百年至一一○○年；其後由中古高地德語（Middle High German）承續，時間為一一○○年至一五○○年，再由現代高地德語（Modern High German）接續至今日，並持續使用與書寫。
凱爾特人。
除了在歷史時期（主要在羅馬統治衰落之後）佔領不列顛的低地日耳曼語外，另一支雅利安語族分支在史前時代遍佈這些島嶼，即凱爾特語。凱爾特人同樣來自大陸，我們發現他們從東向西遷徙穿越高盧和西班牙，偶爾突進巴爾幹半島和意大利半島，並向遠至小亞細亞加拉太地區派出了一個殖民地。

凱爾特語族分為兩個分支：威爾斯語族（Cymric）和蓋爾語族（Goidelic）。前者包括威爾斯語、已消亡的康沃爾語，以及布列塔尼的阿莫瑞卡語；後者包括愛爾蘭語、蘇格蘭蓋爾語和馬恩島語。此外還有古高盧的銘文，有時被視為第三分支即高盧語。凱爾特語族對於民族志學和文法學目的而言誠然意義重大，然而其文獻成書時間較晚，不早於西元八世紀。凱爾特的神話與古代宗教內容顯然已經歷基督教與羅曼文明的過濾，因此在比較研究的目的上必須極其謹慎地運用。

下一類已同樣抵達不列顛島岸邊的雅利安語族，乃為意大利語族。羅馬的文學語言不過是雅利安人定居義大利時所發展的數種方言中之一。除了拉丁文外，我們還發現了奧斯坎語和翁布里亞語，以及數種較小的方言，其中我們擁有紀念性的片段文獻。達到其古典頂峰後，拉丁文成為了西歐文明地帶的通俗語言（lingua vulgaris），並在義大利、高盧、西班牙、葡萄牙、格勞邦登州以及經由殖民在羅馬尼亞發展出新的通俗語言，其後演變成文學語言。我們擁有法文最早的文獻見於九世紀，普羅旺斯語見於十世紀，義大利語、西班牙語及葡萄牙語見於十二世紀。
英格蘭的語言曾兩度被拉丁河的波瀾所觸及，第一次是通過佔領不列顛的羅馬軍團，第二次是通過諾曼征服者——日耳曼血統與斯堪地那維亞血脈的戰士——他們在征服諾曼地後，已將日耳曼語言換為北高盧的語言。他們帶進英格蘭的是羅曼語言、羅曼思想、羅曼風俗與品味，但羅曼血液甚少。英格蘭的日耳曼血液中或許混有某些凱爾特成分；但英語的語法——其血脈——自始至終都保持了日耳曼的特質。
參見萊斯教授，希伯特講座，1886年。
希臘文明。

下一類別是赫臘語族（Hellenic）。在此我們必須謹慎對待一個曾經相當普遍的觀點，即認為遷居至希臘與義大利的雅利安人比雅利安族系中的其他分支更為密切相關。許多學者甚至假定希臘人與羅馬人的祖先在與其餘族群分離後，曾經有一段時間保持聯繫。然而，至少就語言而論，這一假說毫無根據。希臘語與梵語的相似性大於希臘語與拉丁語的相似性，拉丁語與凱爾特語的相似性大於拉丁語與希臘語的相似性。這一點對於我們的神話學與宗教學研究具有重大意義。在有歷史記載的時代，拉丁語言與羅馬神話和宗教從希臘借入了大量內容，以至於學者們往往忽略了一個事實，即借入者並非一無所有，實際上在與希臘接觸之前，義大利就已經存在一個充分發展的宗教與神話體系，而正是義大利生活的這一史前階段對於古代民俗學研究者最具重要意義。
赫臘方言類在其四種方言──多利安方言、埃奧利亞方言、雅典方言與愛奧尼亞方言──中已廣為人知，在此無須贅述。
斯拉夫語族。
我們在歐洲還有雅利安語言的最後一類，即斯拉夫語族，或者如我所傾向的稱法，溫德語族。我傾向於溫德語族這個名稱，因為那些說這些MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF NATURAL RELIGION. 293語言的民族為我們所知的最古老名稱，並非斯拉夫人（Slaves），而是溫德人（Winidae）。
本類分為三個分支：萊特支、東南斯拉夫支及西斯拉夫支。
萊特語族包括（1）萊特語，目前在俄羅斯的庫爾蘭和利沃尼亞波羅的海諸省使用。其文獻可溯至十六世紀。
（2）立陶宛語在東普魯士及俄羅斯地區使用，約有一百萬人使用此語言。其文獻記錄始於1547年，我們現存有該年份的一部簡明教理問答。
（三）古普魯士語，於十七世紀滅絕，僅留下十五、十六世紀的若干片段文獻。
南東斯拉夫語族包括古保加利亞語，我們擁有九世紀的聖經譯本，至今仍被用作「公認譯本」；斯洛文尼亞語、塞爾維亞語和克羅地亞語（有時歸併在伊利里亞語的共同名稱之下），保有十世紀的文學遺存；以及俄語——俄羅斯帝國的文學語言。
西斯拉夫語族包括波蘭語（其文學傳統可溯至十四世紀）、波希米亞語（其文學傳統可溯至十世紀），以及至今仍為盧薩蒂亞的溫德人和蘇布人所使用的各種方言。
西北部分
我們迄今為止所檢視的這五類雅利安語言都屬於歐洲，合起來構成了我所謂的雅利安語族的西北分支。

人們曾多次嘗試證明，在雅利安人定居於現在的各自領地之前，其中某些族群曾長時間聚居在一起，因此共享某些特徵，而這些特徵在其他族群中則付之闕如。在我看來，所有這些嘗試都徒勞無功，而支持所謂雅利安語言「譜系樹」的所有證據，若我們承認以下事實，便可以得到充分說明：辯證方言變種在雅利安人分離之前就已存在，這些方言後來演化成民族語言；無論何種形式對某個或某些氏族最為適合，便得以保存下來；但一旦家族分裂之後，每種方言便各走各的路，對鄰近的方言漠不關心。其他任何假說所製造的困難都與其欲解決的困難一樣多。地理接近與語法相似毫無關聯，這一點在希臘語和拉丁語中最為明顯——雖然這兩種語言的使用者為近鄰，但它們實際上與任何其他兩種雅利安語言一樣各異其趣。凱爾特語與拉丁語共享某些特徵，拉丁語與希臘語如此，條頓語與萊特語亦如此，然而這些偶然的相合無一需要超出「共同的方言發酵」這一解釋——此種發酵先於民族語言的形成而存在，在此處與他處均然。
東南分支。
然而，尤其出於比較的目的，區別構成雅利安語族西北分支的那五個分支，與由印度和波斯諸語言組成的東南分支，仍是有益的。有一種語言現在被認為在這兩個分支之間佔據中間地位，即亞美尼亞語，但其確切的關係仍是爭議的問題。
為什麼這種分為西北支和東南支的劃分在歷史上是有益的，我們在考慮雅利安族在分離前已達到何種智識水平的問題時就會看到。既然在歷史時代中，向東南方向遷往波斯和印度的雅利安人與向西北方向進往歐洲的雅利安人之間顯然沒有發生任何交流，那麼他們所共有的每個詞語，尤其是所有與神話和宗教相關的詞語，都可以被主張為雅利安種族在首次分散前的共同財產。
屬於這個東南分支的語言對我們具有特殊的興趣，因為主要的聖書都是用這些語言所著述的。歐洲從未產生過任何宗教。所有宗教都搖籃於東方，而印度與波斯的語言已成為世界上三大宗教的載體——婆羅門教、佛教（分為南傳佛教、北傳佛教與耆那教三支），以及祆教。因此，這些語言將需要更仔細的考量。
印度等級。吠陀讚歌。
讓我們從印度開始。印度語言最古老的文獻紀念碑是《吠陀》。有趣的是，無論我們在何處發現聖典，它們都向我們呈現了該國最古老的語言。在印度確實如此，

在波斯、中國、巴勒斯坦都是如此，在阿拉伯也大致相同。《吠陀》據稱成書於公元前一千五百年左右，其如何得以保存至今是一則類似童話的故事，但因我們目前只關切語言的歷史，我必須暫時擱置此問題；待我們考察研究自然宗教（Natural Religion）的第四類材料——即《聖典》（Sacred Books）——時，我們將不得不加以考慮。《吠陀》的語言當然曾經是撰作吠陀讚頌詩的人們的活用語言，很可能在印度西北部。但在印度歷史上，該語言始終是聖典語言，擁有許多在後期梵語中聞所未聞的詞彙、文法形式與句法構造。
梵天（Brahma）。
語言的下一個階段仍然是吠陀語，但吠陀讚歌全部採取韻文形式，而下一階段則呈現給我們梵書（Brāhmaṇa）的散文，這些著作旨在闡釋吠陀讚歌與吠陀祭祀。這些梵書的梵語比讚歌的梵語更加定型與規律，但它仍然代表著一個語言時期，先於帕尼尼（Pāṇini）的語法所預設的時期，或者說，先於過去所謂的古典梵語（classical Sanskrit）。
經文（Sutras）。
梵語的下一個階段是蘇陀羅（Sûtras）時期，它在某些方面異於帕尼尼（Pâṇini）所認可的規範梵語，但卻極其接近，以至於兩者之間的時間間隔只能是極為短暫的。

整個這些文獻被保存至今，經歷了三個階段，純粹是祭司文獻，而且乍看之下幾乎令人難以置信的是，這整個文獻在相當長的時期內只靠口頭傳統而得以保存。這些讚頌詩必定在很早的時期就成為最仔細研究的對象。不僅每個字，而且每個字母與每個音調都在各校的教學中得到規定，唯一值得驚奇的是，應當有那麼多不規則的形式能夠逃脫歷代教師的齊一化影響。儘管存在這些不規則性，我們所認識的吠陀語言顯然經過了文法規範的訓練，我們實際上擁有一些著作，其年代可追溯至第三或蘇特拉（Sutra）時期，即所謂的《護持經》（Pratisakhyas），這些著作向我們展示了梨俱吠陀的讚頌詩曾以何等非凡的詳細程度遭到分析。
帕尼尼梵文（Pa/'inean Sanskrit）
隨著《經部》（Sutras）的出現，吠陀語言的這股思想之流告一段落。通常被認為成書於公元前四世紀的波你尼（Pāṇini）著名的文法書，已將吠陀梵文視為特殊且古舊之物，並預設了一種性質迥異的語言和文獻的存在。
我們必須永遠不忘，在古代，文獻一般只為我們提供單一方言的標本，而這種文學方言由於被提升出活生生的語言流，便成為所謂的「古典的」（classical），亦即停滯而死寂的。其他非文學方言則從我們的視野中退隱，但若經過一段時間，一種新語言浮現於表面，並帶來新的文獻——
新的語言往往是舊的古典語言的姐妹方言，而非其直接後代。帕尼尼的規則所適用的語言並非吠陀梵文，而是與之密切相關的梵文。從帕尼尼時代至今日，梵文作為一種新的文學語言，始終保持完全的靜止狀態，理由很簡單：任何對帕尼尼規則的違反，任何對他所確立的古典類型的偏離，在當時曾被視為、且至今仍被視為文法錯誤。
頻毗娑羅王銘文，西元前三世紀

如果我們只透過這兩個管道認識印度語言——吠陀語與帕尼尼語——一切都將是可理解的。但令人驚異的是，當我們第一次接觸到印度口語的歷史文獻時，該語言竟然完全不同。印度最初真正的歷史文獻是公元前三世紀中葉皮亞達西或阿育王（Piyadasi or Asoka）的銘文。這些銘文現在擺在我們面前，保持著當時書寫的樣貌。它們包含的詔令意在讓人民理解，我們可以安心地推想，組成這些銘文的語言若經宣讀，必然對人民來說是可以理解的。
我無法更好地描述那種語言的狀況，除非把它比作梵語的一種口頭俗語方言，就如同義大利語是拉丁語的一種口頭俗語方言。因此，當吠陀梵語和帕尼尼梵語向我們呈現兩種古老的方言──經由細心的文法研究而規範化，並為文學用途而保留──這些西元前三世紀的銘文則向我們代表著人民的活語方言，經由音韻磨耗而淪為其昔日形貌的虛影。
而且不僅如此。梵語的吠陀與帕尼尼（Panini）時代的梵語雖然因規則而呈現劃一的樣貌，但這些銘文中所記錄的語言卻允許無限多樣的變化，這種變化在任何純粹的文學語言中都無法容許。我們在此看到的是印度在西元前第三世紀時實際使用的語言，其發現對於信奉單一古典梵語的人而言實在不是個小驚喜。
佛教梵文。

這還不是全部。當婆羅門教拒絕在宗教事務上使用除梵文以外的任何語言時，那時印度方興未艾的上升宗教佛教，則利用日常方言來影響廣大人民；因此我們發現佛教聖典的一個集合，通常稱為北傳者，係以一種不規則的方言撰寫，極其相似於阿育王碑銘的方言，而第二個集合，通常稱為南傳者，則以另一種通俗方言撰寫，但因其明顯經過較為謹慎的語法潤飾，本質上與前者有根本的差異。前一種方言一般稱為笈多方言（Gāthā dialect）或混合梵文，後者稱為巴利語（Pali），可稱為摩揭陀語（Māgadhī），不過不應將其與同名的後期普拉克里特方言相混淆。這兩種方言我們可以確定其歷史
從歷史的角度看，至少就此而言，我們可以將用迦陀方言（Gatha）編纂的文獻定年於基督紀元前，因為我們擁有大約同一時期北方正典某些著作的漢文譯本。南方正典在口頭傳承多時之後，於西元前88年被記錄成文字。¹ 南方巴利文本與北方迦陀文本最主要的區別在於，前者顯然經歷了嚴格的語法審訂，而後者則未曾如此。
梵文文獻的文藝復興
在西元一世紀末之後，梵語——亦即巴尼尼梵語（Pawinean Sanskrit）——越來越受到重視，我們看到它被用於日常生活的尋常用途，同樣也用於公開銘文。我們通常所理解的梵語文學始於西元四百年左右，而原始語言（Prakrit）方言的文法規範化亦可追溯至大約相同的時期。
普拉克里特語（Prakrit）。

這些俗語方言很可能是那些非文法性方言的直系後裔，這些方言保存在阿育王（Asoka）的銘文中，以及北傳佛教經典的某些文獻裡。但是，儘管當時它們像野生植物一樣生長，現在已經被修剪、整理，並按照帕尼尼（Pacini）文法的模式受到了嚴格的文法規則的約束。以這種形式，它們被用於梵文戲劇中，就像 *Vinaya-pitaka*（律藏），見《東方聖書集成》（Sacred Books of the East），第十三卷，第三十五頁。¹
自然宗教研究之材料。301 正如義大利方言在《即興喜劇》(Commedia dell'arte) 中的運用一樣，其中醫生總是說波隆那方言，丑角說柏格莫方言，帕坦特洛說威尼斯方言，而純粹的托斯卡納方言或羅馬方言則被保留給愛人和情人使用¹。
民族語言
但同時，雖然古典梵語與現在同樣古典的普拉克里特語（Prakrit）自此之後保持靜止不變，語言的古老泉源卻未曾枯竭，反而主要沿著兩條偉大的渠道流動——西方與東方，前者在我們時代以信德語、古吉拉特語、旁遮普語和西方印地語代表，後者則由比哈爾語、孟加拉語、奧里亞語和阿薩姆語代表。北方的尼泊爾語表現出與西方分支更多的親緣性，南方的馬拉地語則與東方分支更多的親緣性。
聖典

我們必須將語言成長與分化的這個輪廓清晰地保持在心中，因為我們將要處理的聖書，如同在這棵言語之樹的枝條上生長一樣。我們擁有吠陀的頌歌、梵書（Brahmanas）及蘇多羅（Sutras）保存於吠陀梵文中。我們擁有摩奴法典（Law Book of Manu）與往世書（Purāṇas）以文學梵文按照帕尼尼（Pāṇini）的模式編纂而成。我們擁有南傳佛教的巴利文聖典、耆那教的經誦（Aṅgas）以古馬哈剌施特里語（old Mahārāṣṭrī）寫成，以及北傳佛教的聖典以非規範的俗語（Prakrit）寫成。我們將看到語言的成長與分化之間甚至存在著某種平行對應。
語言的發展與宗教的發展息息相關，若不了解語言的歷史演變，印度各宗教歷史中的許多要點將會難以理解。

在雅利安語系的伊朗語族中，我們也發現了「特尤斯」（Dyaus）這個名稱，但在此處，它是以「阿胡拉‧馬茲達」（Ahura Mazda）這個更為抽象且崇高的概念形式出現的。這個名稱在《阿維斯塔》（Avesta）中意為「智慧之主」，它代表了對無限者（the Infinite）的一種更為純粹的觀念，幾乎完全擺脫了神話學的具體形象。然而，即便在瑣羅亞斯德（Zarathustra）的改革中，我們仍能辨識出古老的自然崇拜痕跡，例如對「密特拉」（Mithra）——光明的化身——以及「哈歐瑪」（Haoma）——神聖的飲料——的崇敬。這些元素顯示，伊朗語族與印度雅利安人共享了共同的宗教遺產，但後者發展出更為複雜的多神教（polytheism）體系，而前者則傾向於一種近乎一神教（monotheism）的二元論。值得注意的是，在《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中，我們看到「提婆」（Deva）一詞仍保留其「光明者」的原始意義，而在伊朗語族中，同源的「迪弗」（Daeva）卻貶義化為「邪魔」。這種語義的轉變，正是穆勒（Müller）所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）的典型案例，它揭示了宗教觀念如何在歷史進程中發生根本性的變遷。
雅利安族系中我們尚待檢視的最後一類是伊朗族。在此我們發現與印度地區大致相同的現象。該語言最古老的標本見於波斯的聖書《阿維斯陀經》。它被稱為祆教語（Zend），儘管這個名稱完全是一個誤稱，但它可能會保持作為公認的名稱。據推測，此古老方言極有可能是媒體地區的方言，而非波斯的方言。
楔形文波斯銘文。
然而，當我們從當代文獻中首次瞥見波斯語言時——我指的是居魯士、大流士及薛西斯的楔形文字銘文——我們發現那裡的語言雖與瑣羅亞斯德的聖典文字密切相關，卻又有所不同。這些銘文涵蓋了阿契美尼德王朝從約西元前500年至前336年的時期。
巴列維文。

其後出現了超過五個世紀的中斷；但當我們在西元第三世紀上半葉薩珊王朝時期再次遇見新的文獻時，當時稱為帕拉維語（Pehlevi）的語言乃是衰頹的波斯語，不再以楔形文字書寫，而是採用閃語族字母及音節文字。帕拉維文獻主要關注《阿維斯塔》（Avesta）的詮釋與宗教問題，延續至西元約九百年。自西元一千年起，現代波斯語開始出現，我們在菲爾多西（Firdusi）的偉大史詩《列王紀》（Šāhnāmeh）中見到其純正形式，而在其後的時代，因穆罕默德宗教的影響，它愈來愈多地混入阿拉伯詞彙。
這些是雅利安語族的主要語言，也是我們在宗教研究中特別感興趣的語言。還有一些其他語言，如亞洲的亞美尼亞語和奧塞梯語，以及歐洲的阿爾巴尼亞語，這些語言顯然源於雅利安血統，但迄今為止還沒有以完全精確的方式被歸入該語族的任何主要分類。現代阿爾巴尼亞語被認為代表了古代伊利里亞語。亞美尼亞語可能構成一門獨立的語言，據希布施曼（Hiibschmann）所示，它與西北分支的關係比與東南分支的關係更密切。
巴斯克語和伊特魯斯坎語。
在我們離開雅利安語族之前，應當還提及兩種語言，雖然其性質並非雅利安語言，但卻被四面八方的雅利安語使用者所包圍，幾乎要被他們所同化——即巴斯克人（Basks）與伊特魯裡亞人（Etruscans）的語言。
巴斯克人儘管在語言學目的上令人感興趣，但在其古代宗教的性質方面卻提供了我們甚少的資訊。相反地，伊特魯里亞人留給了我們大量的文物與銘文資料，然而必須坦承，除非發現了一把真正可靠的鑰匙來解開他們的語言，否則我們不會有任何機會去理解其宗教的真正特質。
閃語族
完全獨立於這股主宰印度、波斯、亞美尼亞及歐洲大部分地區的龐大語言洪流之外，還有另一股語言洪流——閃語族，自始便在其自有的河床中流動，滋養著世界上兩種（如果不是三種）偉大宗教，即猶太教、基督教及穆罕默德教。
閃語族可分為三個分支，即亞蘭語、希伯來語及阿拉伯語；或分為兩個分支，北方分支包括亞蘭語及希伯來語，南方分支則為阿拉伯語。
阿拉美文。

亞蘭語包括亞述和巴比倫的古代語言，就其在楔形文字銘文中所被發現並譯解的範圍而言。這種古代語言的文法結構尚未充分被闡明，以至於學者們還無法追溯其與後來亞蘭語的確切關係。不過，就地理位置而言，美索不達米亞的古代語言目前可暫時被歸類為亞蘭語。若如某些學者所主張，這些楔形文字銘文的某些可回溯到公元前四千年，它們將代表閃語族語言最古老的遺存。而若如似乎普遍承認的那樣，該閃語族文學之前曾有另一種文明存在，非閃語族，且通常被稱為蘇美-阿卡德文明（Sumero-Accadian），我們將得以洞悉一個遠比埃及和中國所宣稱的時代更為久遠的過去。事情可能確實如此，但即使從年代學角度言，巴比倫聖歌所傳達給我們的宗教觀念應該證明遠早於任何雅利安種族的觀念，我必須立即聲明，在我看來它們顯得遠為先進，就文明程度而言遠為現代。它們預設了城鎮、廟宇、偶像、金屬和各類寶石的知識、對書寫的熟悉，以及許多我們在吠陀讚歌中將徒勞無功地尋找的抽象觀念。就語言學而言，這些銘文中也幾乎沒有什麼我們會認為比敘利亞語、阿拉伯語或希伯來語的文法結構更為原始的內容。許多困難在此仍須澄清。然而，一個重要的宗教研究寶庫無疑已被開啟，而希伯來傳統的若干前驅已在巴比倫楔形文字文獻中被發現。若如我們所讀，亞伯拉罕來自迦勒底的烏爾，他的語言應該與楔形文字銘文的語言相近。但他的名字及與他相關的一切在後來時代卻經過了另一種語言的途徑，我們現在稱之為希伯來語；而無論他的事蹟如何被記錄成現今我們所擁有的那種形式的時代仍然不確定，但無論如何遠晚於從前所認為的時間。
迦勒底語與敘利亞語。
在歷史時期，我們發現亞蘭語在巴比倫和亞述王國中使用，並從那裡傳播到敘利亞和巴勒斯坦。由於這些王國的政治和文學優勢，亞蘭語似乎在某段時期成為一種通用語言（lingua franca），其影響力擴及波斯、敘利亞、小亞細亞、埃及，甚至阿拉伯。
通常的做法是將雅利安人、閃語族人和圖蘭語族人區分開來。306 第十一講
猶太人使用的亞蘭文，以及基督教作家在後來時代使用的亞蘭文，前者被稱為迦勒底文，後者為敍利亞文。誠然，迦勒底這個名稱的起源，可能源於這樣一種錯誤的觀念，即認為該語言是由巴比倫流亡歸返的猶太人引入巴勒斯坦的。但這個名稱已經沿用已久，以至於用「西方亞蘭文」這樣的新名稱來取代它已無足輕重。

這種猶太迦勒底語首先出現在《舊約》的一些書籍中，例如《以斯拉記》和《但以理書》。此後，我們在塔古姆（Targums）或迦勒底文的五經譯本（翁克洛斯譯本）和先知書譯本（喬納森譯本）中找到它，這些譯本在被最終編訂於公元四至五世紀之前，早已在會堂中被誦讀。《耶路撒冷塔古姆》和《耶路撒冷塔木德》呈現了當時猶太人在耶路撒冷和加利利所使用的迦勒底語。基督和他的門徒必定使用過同樣的亞蘭語方言，儘管他們在對廣大民眾講話時也使用希臘語。阿拉伯人的征服和他們語言的傳播早在七世紀就干擾了迦勒底語的文學培養；但某些猶太作家仍持續使用它直到十世紀。
撒瑪利亞人將五經翻譯成自己的亞蘭方言，該方言與猶太人的方言差異甚微。
曼德安人（Mandaeans）是巴比倫尼亞一支頗為混雜的基督教派，他們使用並書寫迦勒底方言，這種方言保存在他們的著作中，以及現存該派少數成員的行話裡。
敘利亞語雖然在基督教興起之前早已通行，但其文學上的開發主要還是由基督教作家們推動的。舊約與新約聖經在第二世紀被譯成敘利亞語（佩希托譯本），並成為埃德薩學派及其他地方公認的文本。自第三世紀至第七世紀，一大批文獻積累而成，其影響力擴展至波斯及東羅馬帝國。然而，阿拉伯征服終結了這種語言的文學開發。雖然它作為書寫方言與口語方言一直延續到十二世紀，並在此後作為學者的語言存續至今，但其盛況已不復再。
新敘利亞方言仍在美索不達米亞的某些地區使用，主要由景教徒在摩蘇爾周邊及庫爾德斯坦直至烏爾米亞湖地帶使用，這些方言並非直接源自文語敘利亞語，而是代表古亞蘭語口語的殘存。其中一種方言近來因基督教傳教士的努力，而獲得了一定程度的文學開發。
希伯來的。
第二分支為希伯來語族，包括腓尼基語和迦太基語——我們對它們的認識來自約西元前600年的銘文——以及舊約聖經的希伯來語。
摩押人說希伯來語，從大約西元前九百年梅沙王的銘文可以看出這一點。腓尼基人似乎也說同樣的語言，儘管可能帶有方言差異。到了馬加比時代左右，希伯來語及其相關方言便不再是人民普遍使用的語言了，儘管希伯來語仍然保持著語言
在提圖斯摧毀耶路撒冷之後很久，這種語言仍被學者們使用。甚至到了現在，猶太人在文學討論和相互交往中仍然使用一種人工的、訛變的希伯來文。
阿拉伯語。

第三個分支是阿拉伯語，其發源地在阿拉伯半島，至今仍為當地大多數居民所使用，並自此向亞洲、非洲及歐洲傳播，此乃穆罕默德征服時代之事。早在穆罕默德之前，阿拉伯就已有通俗文學存在（Mo'allakat），而在希賈茲北部發現的銘文，通稱為撒穆德銘文（Thamudic），據信年代早於基督教時代。阿拉伯銘文持續被發現，證明阿拉伯語在穆罕默德時代之久遠前即已作為一種文明語言而被使用。札巴德（Zabad）的三語銘文（亞拉姆語、阿拉伯語、希臘語）年代為西元五一三年；哈蘭（Harran）的雙語銘文（阿拉伯語和希臘語）年代為西元五六八年。穆罕默德及其後繼者所宣揚的新宗教為阿拉伯人的文學生活帶來了新的推動力。《古蘭經》的語言在古代貝都因詩歌之外形成了文學卓越的新典範。在聖遷（Hejra）後的第二個世紀，語法研究確立了古典阿拉伯語的規則，並且在此後的一千二百年間，所有受過教育的阿拉伯人仍能誦讀並理解《古蘭經》。然而，口語阿拉伯語在埃及、阿爾及利亞、敘利亞及阿拉伯各地的方言差異甚大。在馬爾他仍有一種阿拉伯方言持續被使用。
撒巴亞或希姆葉里特。

在阿拉伯半島南部存在著古代薩巴亞文明的遺跡，可作為研究自然宗教的材料。這些遺跡已在巨大的紀念碑和眾多銘文中被發現，這些銘文以一種特殊的字母書寫，通常稱為希姆亞爾字母（Himyaritic）。其年代難以確定，但其中一些遺跡據推測可溯及我們所處時代之前，並延伸至西元四世紀。從這些薩巴亞銘文中可以區辨出不同方言的蹤跡，但它們都與阿拉伯語密切相關。薩巴亞語在阿拉伯半島南部可能一直被使用到穆罕默德教的傳來，該宗教使阿拉伯語成為整個葉門的通用語言。
衣索比亞語。
在很早的年代，似乎有一支來自阿拉伯，或更準確地說來自薩巴亞（Sabaea）的殖民者跨越至非洲。在此地，即埃及及努比亞之南，一種古老而極其原始的閃語族方言保留至今日，該方言與薩巴亞及阿拉伯語密切相關，此即埃塞俄比亞語或阿比西尼亞語，或稱吉茲語（Geez）。我們擁有第三或第四世紀的埃塞俄比亞語聖經譯本。其後陸續出現其他著作，其性質均為神學著述。
在古代衣索比亞語中也有銘文，年代可追溯至阿克蘇姆王國時期，大約在西元三百五十年至五百年之間。
這種古代衣索比亞語在第九世紀已不再作為日常用語使用，但它在很長一段時間內仍保有文學語言的地位。
自十五、十六世紀始，出現了一種新的語言——阿姆哈拉語。在這種語言中，閃語族的類型已遭到強烈的改變，這種現象很可能是由於說這種語言的部族具有哈姆語族的血統所致。
起源。它是一種傳播的語言，在現代已經產生了新的文學。
其他方言，如提格雷語（Tigre）、哈迪亞語（Ehdli）和哈拉里語（Harrari），均以其使用地區命名，迄今尚未經過充分探究，閃語族學者無法判斷它們究竟是阿姆哈拉語的變體，或是更古老的獨立方言的代表¹。

閃語族語言的家族相似性絲毫不亞於雅利安語族，甚至更為強烈。其語音特徵以咽頭音的優勢地位為標誌，其詞源特徵則表現在多數詞根的三輔音形式，以及這些詞根通過代詞性後綴與前綴的變化方式；其文法特徵則在於元音的固定性用以表達意義的主要變化，這種固定性使得省略元音符號書寫成為可能。這些特徵性特點發展得如此充分，足以使人完全不可能想像閃語族語言曾經源自雅利安語族，或雅利安語族曾源自閃語族。兩者是否都可能源自一個共同的來源，這是一個經常被提出的問題，一般來說都是根據個人的偏好而回答的。我認為大多數學者都會承認，無法證明遠古時代的共同起源是完全不可能的。然而，支持與反對這一觀點的證據本質上都是如此難以捉摸、稍縱即逝，以至於不在實用語言學的範疇之內。
關於閃語族語言最新且最佳的說明，由諾爾德克在《大英百科全書》中提供。
